experienced stigma: I went to a family dinner over the weekend and as soon as I checked my blood sugar, my aunt loud enough for everyone to hear said, "I knew you'd end up with diabetes eating all that junk when you were younger." Everyone just sort of nodded and got quiet. It made me feel like no matter how hard I try now, I’ll never escape how they look at me.

At a family gathering yesterday, my aunt loudly announced to everyone that I *"brought this diabetes on myself"* by being "too lazy and eating junk food". She ignores the real medical issues and makes me feel embarrassed in front of everyone just because I have diabetes.

Today at work I overheard my manager reminding my coworkers about the health program our office is running. When I asked about joining, they politely told me “Oh, this is meant to prevent diabetes—not for people who already have it, sorry!” Sometimes it feels like everyone just automatically assumes my case is the result of my own choices and that it’s too late for support. A little compassion would go a long way.

At a family gathering earlier, someone loudly said, "You brought cupcakes? But you’re diabetic, why are you tempting the rest of us anyways?" Everyone just stared and started whispering about how irresponsible I was. It made me feel both embarrassed and angry, all just because I brought dessert like everyone else.

Last week at a family dinner, my aunt found out I needed to inject insulin before eating and she looked at me in front of everyone and said, "Maybe if you took better care of yourself when you were younger, you wouldn’t need those needles." It was humiliating not just because she blamed me but because it seemed everyone agreed, silently nodding their heads or avoiding eye contact. Sometimes I feel like I’ll never escape this shame just for having diabetes.

Last time I visited my endocrinologist, she barely looked at my charts and told me, “Well, maybe if you lost some weight you wouldn't be struggling to control your blood sugar.” It felt like she completely overlooked the effort I make and just blamed me for having diabetes. No matter what I say, I'm always treated as if it’s my fault for living with this condition.

Went into a bakery yesterday to grab something for a friend’s birthday, and when I mentioned I couldn’t have the cookies myself because of diabetes, one of the staff just looked at me and said “Shouldn’t you stay out of here then?” in front of everyone. Felt so unnecessary and honestly just made me want to crawl away.

Last time I went to the dentist, she said she "hates seeing diabetics" because “they always have gum problems” and made me feel really uncomfortable about my health. It's so discouraging getting this reaction from medical professionals who are supposed to help, not judge.

I was at a dinner with some friends, and when I pulled out my insulin to give myself a dose before eating, someone across the table said loudly, "Are you sure you need that much? Diabetics bring it on themselves with bad choices." I felt so embarrassed—everyone went quiet and all eyes were on me, like I was doing something wrong by taking care of myself. Made me want to stop going out altogether.

Out shopping yesterday and I checked my sugars in front of some friends. One of them made a big show of moving away from me and said in front of everyone, “You’re just like my aunt, probably brought it on yourself with bad choices.” Felt super embarrassed and so tired of people acting like having diabetes is something shameful or contagious.
perceived stigma: Sometimes it feels like whenever I mention being diabetic, people look at me like I've failed at living healthy. It's as if there's this judgment in the air that I must have done something wrong to end up with this condition. Even when I say it's type 1, folks assume bad habits are the only route to diabetes. Does anyone feel irritated by strangers' assumptions, or is it just me?

Sometimes I feel like people look at me and just assume I brought diabetes on myself — like every doctor’s warning about “bad habits” must automatically fit me. It’s lonely behind the judgment. If I so much as eat a piece of fruit, there’s a sense that people think I’m breaking some rule. Diabetes isn’t a character flaw, it’s part of my biology — but not everyone sees it that way.

Sometimes it feels like no matter what I do, people just assume I caused my diabetes. I've overheard coworkers whisper things like "well, you know, if she just ate better..." It's like people treat diabetes as a punishment for something you did wrong, instead of an illness that anyone could have.

It's exhausting how some people assume I must have "let myself go" if they find out I have diabetes. It feels like whenever it comes up, someone’s ready with a judgment or a lecture about my habits. I get embarrassed even bringing it up, because I'm convinced people just see the diagnosis as something I brought on myself. Does anyone else struggle with the shame of not just the illness, but of being misunderstood all the time?

Sometimes it feels like people just assume if you have diabetes you must have done something wrong, or that you just let yourself go. The unspoken judgment weighs on you every time someone hears the word “diabetes” and goes silent, or starts offering you diet advice like you don’t have a clue. It’s like you have to fight their opinion before you even explain your own experience.

Usually when people hear you have diabetes they assume it’s because you made bad choices or aren’t disciplined. It feels like everyone thinks it’s your own fault, and that just makes opening up so much harder.

Sometimes I feel like everyone judges me the second they see my insulin pump or that I turn down dessert. Like people think I made terrible choices to “deserve” this, even though it's just part of who I am and I do my best every day. Wish folks could understand it’s not about blame—it’s about dealing with the hand you’re dealt.

I hate how every time I tell someone I have diabetes, they assume it must be because I ate too much sugar or didn’t exercise. It’s not that simple at all—there are so many factors, but it feels like people are just judging without knowing anything about the condition.

Sometimes I feel like people hear I have diabetes and automatically think I've messed something up in my life—it seems like everyone secretly blames you for your health. It's exhausting always having to defend myself when I mention what I can or can't eat around others.

Sometimes I feel like people around me think getting diabetes was some kind of personal failure on my part, like I must’ve done something wrong or been “bad.” It’s exhausting trying to explain that it’s not that simple. The judgment gets old when you realize people actually believe these stereotypes.
anticipated stigma: I struggle a lot with deciding when and how to let people know I have diabetes, especially when meeting new friends or at work events. I always worry in advance that if I have to test my blood sugar or take insulin in front of them, they'll start judging me, treating me differently, or assuming stuff about my health. It almost always stops me from saying anything at all, and then later I end up stressed about not being open. Anyone else stress about those initial conversations, or how people might react?

I've just started dating someone new and I'm honestly a little stressed about eventually having "the talk" about my diabetes. I know it's just part of who I am, but I can't help but worry they'll see me differently or not understand. I could try hiding all my testing stuff or alarms pay or act like nothing’s wrong, but sooner or later it always needs to come up. Having to think about how they might react is draining honestly – worried they’ll look at me like I’m fragile or sick or just get turned off. Don't mean to overthink it but it feels like a big thing looming over everything good right now.

I'm starting a new semester at college and a part of me is anxious every time I walk into a new class with my glucose meter or snacks to treat lows. I keep worrying that someone will see me testing or treating and make assumptions or ask all sorts of questions I don't want to answer. Even thinking about explaining my diabetes to a group project partner just makes me cringe. I'm always anticipating how people might react — I worry they will think I'm somehow less capable when they notice, so I just try to hide it as much as I can.

Taking the bus lately, and I often need to check my blood sugar or give myself insulin while commuting. But every time, I put it off or try to hide it with my jacket because I’m always worried some stranger is going to judge me or make a comment. I wish I could just manage my diabetes without worrying about possibly getting weird looks or having to explain. Anybody else ever feel this way?

I’m meeting new people at work this week and dreading the inevitable questions about my insulin pump. I always get anxious they'll judge me or treat me differently if they find out I have type 1 diabetes, so I'm considering wearing looser shirts just to keep it hidden. I wish it didn't have to be this way, but after previous awkward experiences, I can’t stop worrying about how people will react once they know.

I just started college this year and honestly, I’m worried about what people will think when they see me testing my blood sugar in lectures or eating snacks during class to bring up bad lows. Last week my CGM alarm went off loudly and everyone stared—I could feel my face turning red. I try to be on top of my health, but I also don’t want to be seen as the “diabetes kid” or make it all awkward with my professors or other students. Wish there was a subtle way to do all this, but I can’t help but feel nervous every time I have to do something diabetes-related in front of others.

Getting anxious thinking about having to inject insulin at a family get-together this weekend. I know most people probably won’t say anything, but I really worry about strange looks and having to answer uncomfortable questions for trying to manage my diabetes. Wish I didn’t feel the need to hide it but it's tough.

I have to travel for work next month and I'm really anxious about managing my diabetes in front of my colleagues. I can already imagine the awkward questions or judgment if they see me checking my blood sugar at lunch or needing to treat a low. I just don't want to seem weird or incompetent because of my diabetes, but I also know I have to prioritize my health. Wish I didn't have to feel so stressed just thinking about how others might react.

I'm starting a new semester next week and debating whether or not to tell my group partners about my diabetes. Last year, one classmate kind of freaked out when I needed some juice at an unexpected time, and I still feel self-conscious about it. Now I keep worrying the same thing will happen again, or people will see me as extra work or get awkward (or even avoid working with me). Makes me super nervous to bring it up at all.

Every time I start seeing someone new, I get anxious about when (or if) I should tell them I have diabetes. I worry they’ll see me differently or think it’s going to be a big burden. I want to be honest, but I get stuck wondering: will they start treating me weird if they know?
internalized stigma: I always thought that if I just ate right and exercised, I’d never have to deal with something like diabetes. Now that I have it, I keep asking myself where I messed up and blaming myself every time my blood sugars are high. Some days it feels like I could have prevented this if only I had tried harder. I can’t help but think I let myself down, and some days it really eats at me.

Sometimes I look back on all the food decisions I made over the years and just feel like I did this to myself. Even though I know there are many factors at play, I can’t help the feeling that my diagnosis is somehow my punishment for all my past choices.

I find myself constantly blaming myself for developing diabetes. Every time I look at a prescription or poke my finger to check my blood sugar, I wonder if it's just payback for years of poor choices. I feel like I let myself down and caused this, even when people say it isn’t my fault.

I hate that I let things get to this point. Every checkup feels like another reminder that I should have done better with my eating and exercise. My friends and family try to be encouraging, but it just makes me feel even more like it’s my own fault that I have to live with this disease. It’s so hard not to blame myself for every out-of-range blood sugar or doctor's concern, even though I know there's so much I can't control. I wish I could accept that, instead of just feeling ashamed.

I've been dealing with diabetes for a while now and every time my blood sugars are a little out of range, I can't help but feel like I'm failing myself. No matter how hard I try, I feel ashamed, like it means I'm not doing enough or I'm just not strong enough to manage it perfectly.

Sometimes I think I brought all of this on myself. With my diabetes it just feels like every bad reading is a sign I failed—like if I had done more, or had more willpower, maybe I wouldn’t be in this spot. It's hard not to feel ashamed, even though I know on some level it's not really my fault... but the guilt just never really goes away.

Sometimes I feel like managing my diabetes is all on me and that every time my numbers are out of range, it's entirely my fault for not trying hard enough. Even when I'm making better choices, a bad reading just makes me feel ashamed like I'm letting myself down over and over. I find it hard to talk about this because it seems like if I were just a bit more disciplined or smart with my food decisions none of it would be so hard.

It’s hard not to blame myself for all of this. I look back and wish I’d tried harder to eat better or exercise, maybe things would have been different right now. When I told my friends about the diabetes, I could tell part of them believed I did this to myself, and honestly that hurts, but it’s even worse because I believe it too sometimes. Every time I take my medication or check my blood sugar, it just feels like a failure on my part. I know I need to move forward, but the guilt is overwhelming.

Since being diagnosed with diabetes last year, I keep replaying what I did wrong – too much snacking, should’ve exercised more, refused to see the doctor until it got worse. Part of me feels like I did this to myself, and sometimes I even feel embarrassed to take my medication in front of people. All my friends are so careless when it comes to junk food but somehow they never end up with these problems. I honestly find it really hard not to blame myself for having diabetes at all.

Sometimes I ask myself “Why couldn’t I just control what I ate?” I feel so ashamed whenever I talk about my diabetes—like people are judging, but most of all, I judge myself. I’ve tried so hard but a part of me feels like this whole situation is my punishment for not respecting my body enough before. It’s exhausting constantly feeling like I did this to myself.
intersectional stigma: It’s always assumed that when a Black person has diabetes, it’s because they're lazy or can't control themselves around food. Can't people see that there’s more to it? Tired of acting like our background determines our health struggles.

It feels like people only see my Blackness or my weight when they find out I have diabetes. I just wish they’d understand it’s not only about what I look like, or assume I must have done something wrong. How do you fight being seen as only a set of stereotypes and not as a whole person?

Went to the pharmacy and the tech laughed when I asked about diabetes-friendly snacks. She whispered to a coworker about “crazy folks on all those meds” then stared pointedly at the scars from my schizophrenia on my arms. Gets exhausting always being judged for having BOTH, like it’s my fault. Calling out every bias is a full-time gig these days.

When people see me buying insulin, they automatically think I must’ve done it to myself – first being overweight, now diabetic. Feels worse depending on who’s at the counter too; as a Black woman it feels like I’m being silently judged twice. I just want to manage my health without those looks.

Just tired of doctors blaming all my health problemen on my weight and background— as if being Latina and living with family foods gives me T2D and I’m “just not trying” hard enough 😤 Sometimes it feels like I face twice the judgment, for looking a certain way and for having diabetes…

People act so shocked when you tell them you live with diabetes AND schizophrenia. Like, sorry I wasn't aware I broken both the mind and my body. Seriously, sometimes it feels like the doctor doesn’t see past the “stigma labels”— overweight, ethnic, bipolar — instead of HELPING with some actual care.

My uncle was told it’s his own fault for having diabetes AND schizophrenia—can you imagine? Like the doctor said he probably just ‘doesn’t care about himself’ between struggling from meds that increase blood sugar and hearing demons on the bus. The looks we get when-people spot him checking his glucose, complicated even more by his heavy accent. It never matters what steps you take if you look or live ‘wrong’.

No one is surprised I got type 2 diabetes—they look at my body and think it’s just because I’m fat. Try dealing with stares at the clinic as a Black woman, like everyone decided what diseases I “should” have just based on how I look.

I'm tired of hearing people say, "Of course you got diabetes, just look at what background you're from and your size." It's never just about health – it's like we're judged for everything at once: how we look, where we're from, what we eat. When people find out I have T2D and I'm overweight & not white, seems like they already have their conclusion.
So tired of people just assuming my uncle can’t manage his diabetes because he has schizophrenia and lives in public housing. The judgment is always twice as bad if you’re Black and poor.
